---
title: "Statistical inference for 1D categorical data"
subtitle: "<br>36-315: Statistical Graphics and Visualization, Summer 2026"
footer:  "[36-315-summer26.netlify.app](https://36-315-summer26.netlify.app)"
format:
  revealjs:
    embed-resources: true
    height: 900
    width: 1600
    theme: theme.scss
    smaller: true
    slide-number: c/t
    linestretch: 1.4
    code-line-numbers: false
    html-math-method:
      method: mathjax
      url: "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/mathjax@3/es5/tex-mml-chtml.js"
---

```{r}
#| include: false
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = TRUE,
  message = FALSE,
  warning = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center"
)
ggplot2::theme_set(ggplot2::theme_gray(base_size = 20))
```

## What does a bar chart show?

**Marginal distribution**: probability that a categorical variable $X$ (e.g., `species`) takes each particular category value $x$ (`Adelie`, `Chinstrap`, `Gentoo`)

* Frequency bar charts give info about sample size

```{r}
table(penguins$species)
```

* Now, we create a proportion/percent bar chart to display the individual probabilities

* This shows the probability mass function (PMF) for discrete variables

  * e.g., $P($`species` $=$ `Adelie`$)$
  
```{r}
prop.table(table(penguins$species))
```

## Proportion bar charts

::: columns
::: {.column}

* Compute proportions "by hand" with `count()` and `mutate()`

* Use the pipe operator `|>` to perform multiple operations

* Use `geom_col()`, since we want the heights of the bars to represent values in the data

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
penguins |>
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n))
```

:::

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| fig-height: 8
penguins |>
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = species, y = prop)) +
  geom_col()
```
:::
:::

## Statistical inference for proportions

* Estimate $P($`species` = $C_j$ ) with $\hat p_j$ for each category $C_j$ ( $\hat p_\texttt{Adelie}$, $\hat p_\texttt{Chinstrap}$, $\hat p_\texttt{Gentoo}$ )

```{r}
prop.table(table(penguins$species))
```

* Quantify uncertainty for $\displaystyle \hat p_j = \frac{n_j}{n}$ with the standard error $$\textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}_j(1 - \hat{p}_j)}{n}}$$

* Compute $\alpha$-level **confidence interval** $\hat{p}_j \pm z_{1 - \alpha / 2} \cdot \textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j)$

* Good rule-of-thumb: construct 95% confidence interval using $\hat{p}_j \pm 2 \cdot \textsf{se}(\hat{p}_j)$

:::aside
The CI is based on a normal approximation justfied by the Central Limit Theorem. (Note: the CI could include values outside of $[0,1]$...)<br>To get CI for counts, just multiply $\hat p$ by $n$.
:::

## Adding confidence intervals to bar chart

::: columns
::: {.column}
<br>

```{r}
#| eval: false
penguins |> 
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), 
                color = "blue", 
                width = 0.2, 
                linewidth = 1)
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 10
penguins |> 
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), 
                color = "blue", width = 0.2, linewidth = 1)
```
:::
:::

## Ordering factors in a bar chart

::: columns
::: {.column}
Order the bars by proportion

```{r}
#| eval: false
penguins |> 
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se,
         species = fct_reorder(species, prop)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), 
                color = "blue", 
                width = 0.2, 
                linewidth = 1)
```
:::

::: {.column}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 10
penguins |> 
  count(species) |> 
  mutate(prop = n / sum(n),
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / sum(n)),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se,
         species = fct_reorder(species, prop)) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = prop, y = species)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = lower, xmax = upper), 
                color = "blue", 
                width = 0.2, 
                linewidth = 1)
```
:::
:::

## Don't do this... (Look closely...)

<center>
![](https://retractionwatch.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/12/3802603.fig_.009-768x512.png){height=700}
<center>

## Statistical inference: hypothesis testing in general

Computing $p$-values works like this:

* Choose a test statistic

* Compute the test statistic using the data

* Is test statistic "unusual" compared to what we would expect under the null?

* Compare $p$-value to the target error rate ("significance level") $\alpha$

  * Typically choose $\alpha = 0.05$ ([the origins of 0.05](https://www2.psych.ubc.ca/~schaller/528Readings/CowlesDavis1982.pdf))
  
  * In other words, if we reject the null hypothesis at $\alpha = 0.05$, then, assuming $H_0$ is true, there is a 5% chance it is a false positive (also known as Type 1 error)

## Chi-squared test for 1D categorical data

::: columns
::: {.column}

* Null hypothesis: $H_0$: $p_1 = p_2 = \cdots = p_K$

* Test statistic: $\displaystyle \chi^2 = \sum_{j=1}^K \frac{(O_j - E_j)^2}{E_j}$, where

  * $O_j$: observed counts in category $j$

  * $E_j$ : expected counts under $H_0$<br>(each category is equally likely to occur with probability $n/K = p_1 = p_2 = \cdots = p_K$)
  
:::

::: {.column}

<br>

```{r}
chisq.test(table(penguins$species))
```

:::
:::

## Graphics versus statistical inference

::: columns
::: {.column width="57%"}

Reminder: **Anscombe’s Quartet**

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 8
anscombe |> 
  pivot_longer(everything(),
               cols_vary = "slowest",
               names_to = c(".value", "set"),
               names_pattern = "(.)(.)") |> 
  ggplot(aes(x, y)) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "darkorange", fullrange = TRUE) +
  geom_point(size = 4) +
  facet_wrap(~ set) +
  theme_light(base_size = 20)
```

:::

::: {.column width="43%"}

<br>

* Statistical inference is the same,<br>but the graphics are very different

* The opposite can be true!

* Graphics can be the same,<br>but statistical inference is very different

:::
:::


## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c("A", "A", "B", "C"))
fake_chisq_test_results <- chisq.test(table(fake_counts$fake_group))
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1, 2)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts),
                      "; p-value = ",
                      signif(fake_chisq_test_results$p.value, digits = 2))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 22, hjust = 0.5, color = NA),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 16))
```

## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c("A", "A", "B", "C"))
# Run and store the chi-square test:
fake_chisq_test_results <- chisq.test(table(fake_counts$fake_group))
# Create the plot
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 1, 2)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts),
                      "; p-value = ",
                      signif(fake_chisq_test_results$p.value, digits = 2))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 22, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 16))
```


## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 8), 
                                     rep("B", 4), rep("C", 4)))
# Run and store the chi-square test:
fake_chisq_test_results <- chisq.test(table(fake_counts$fake_group))
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue") +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts),
                      "; p-value = ",
                      signif(fake_chisq_test_results$p.value, digits = 2))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 22, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 16))
```

## Toy example: 3 categories, $p_1 = 1/2,\ p_2 = p_3 = 1/4$

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 32), 
                                     rep("B", 16), rep("C", 16)))
# Run and store the chi-square test:
fake_chisq_test_results <- chisq.test(table(fake_counts$fake_group))
# Create the plot
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue") +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(0, 8, 16, 24, 32)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts),
                      "; p-value = ",
                      signif(fake_chisq_test_results$p.value, digits = 2))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 22, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 16))
```

## Power under this scenario: $2n/4, n/4, n/4$

```{r}
#| echo: false
# Iterate through a sequence of sample sizes to compute the pvalue:
chisq_power_table <- map(
  seq(4, 100, by = 4),
  # Apply the following function to each value
  function(x) {
    # Init the fake data:
    fake_data <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 2 * x / 4),
                                       rep("B", x / 4), rep("C", x / 4)))
    
    # Run and store the chi-square test:
    fake_results <- chisq.test(table(fake_data$fake_group))
    
    # Return a table with the p-value and n:
    tibble("n" = x, "pval" = fake_results$p.value)
  }
) |>
  list_rbind()

# Plot the results:
chisq_power_table |>
  # Note the use of 
  ggplot(aes(x = n, y = pval)) +
  # Draw a line layer to connect the points 
  geom_line(color = "gray", linewidth = 1.5) +
  geom_point(color = "black", size = 3) +
  # Add a horizontal line at 0.05:
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed", 
             color = "darkorange", linewidth = 1.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 100, by = 20)) +
  theme_light() +
  labs(x = "Sample size", y = "p-value") +
  theme(axis.title = element_text(size = 16),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 15))
```

## How do we combine graphics with inference?

* Simply add $p$-values (or other info) to graph via text

* Add confidence intervals to the graph

  * Need to remember what each CI is for
  
  * The CIs on previous slides are for each $\hat{p}_j$ marginally, **NOT** jointly

  * Have to be careful with **multiple testing**

## Confidence intervals visually capture uncertainty

::: columns

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c("A", "A", "B", "C"))
# Compute the summary with standard errors:
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  group_by(fake_group) |>
  summarize(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(total = sum(count),
         prop = count / total,
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / total),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue",
           aes(y = prop),
           stat = "identity") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower,
                    ymax = upper),
                color = "darkorange",
                width = 0.25, 
                linewidth = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 32, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 25))
```

:::

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 32), 
                                     rep("B", 16), rep("C", 16)))# Compute the summary with standard errors:
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  group_by(fake_group) |>
  summarize(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(total = sum(count),
         prop = count / total,
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / total),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue",
           aes(y = prop),
           stat = "identity") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower,
                    ymax = upper),
                color = "darkorange",
                width = 0.25, 
                linewidth = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("n = ", nrow(fake_counts))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 32, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 25))
```

:::

:::

## (Rough) Rules-of-thumb for comparing CIs on bar charts

Comparing overlap of two CIs is **NOT** exactly the same as directly testing for a significant difference...

* Really we want CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ ), not CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ )
  
* CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ ) not overlapping implies $0 \notin$ CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ )

* CI( $\hat{p_1}$ ) and CI( $\hat{p_2}$ ) overlapping **DOES NOT** imply $0 \in$ CI( $\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2$ ) 

. . .

Roughly speaking:

* If CIs don't overlap: significant difference
  
* If CIs overlap a little: ambiguous
  
* If CIs overlap a lot: no significant difference

. . .

But if we're comparing more than two CIs simultaneously, we need to account for **multiple testing**

  * When you look for all non-overlapping CIs: implicitly making $\displaystyle \binom{K}{2}$ pairwise tests in your head

## Corrections for multiple testing

* In those bar plots, when we determine whether CIs overlap, we make 3 comparisons:

  1. A vs B

  2. A vs C

  3. B vs C

. . .
  
* **This is a multiple testing (or multiple comparison) problem**

. . .

* In short: we will make Type 1 errors (chance of false rejection) more than 5% of the time!

* Type 1 error: reject $H_0$ when $H_0$ is true

* e.g., CIs don't overlap but actually $H_0: p_A = p_B$ is true
  
* If only interested in A vs B and nothing else, then just construct 95% CI for A vs B and control error rate at 5%
  
* However, if we construct several CIs, where A vs B is just one comparison we make, our Type 1 error rate is > 5%!

## Corrections for multiple testing

Basic idea:

* Corrections for multiple testing inflate $p$-values (i.e., make them bigger)

* Equivalently, they inflate CIs (i.e., make them wider)

* Goal: control Type 1 error rate $\leq 5\%$

. . .

**Bonferroni correction**, which inflates $p$-values the most but is simple and easy to implement

. . .

* We usually reject null hypothesis when $p$-value $\leq 0.05$

* **Bonferroni**: if making $K$ comparisons, reject only if $p$-value $\leq 0.05/K$

* For CIs: instead of plotting 95% CIs, we plot $(1 - 0.05/K)%$% CIs

  * e.g., when $K = 3$, plot 98.33% CIs
  

## Impact of Bonferroni correction on confidence intervals

::: columns
::: {.column}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 32), 
                                     rep("B", 16), rep("C", 16)))# Compute the summary with standard errors:
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  group_by(fake_group) |>
  summarize(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(total = sum(count),
         prop = count / total,
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / total),
         lower = prop - 2 * se,
         upper = prop + 2 * se) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue",
           aes(y = prop),
           stat = "identity") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower,
                    ymax = upper),
                color = "darkorange",
                width = 0.25, 
                linewidth = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("No correction; n = ", nrow(fake_counts))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 32, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 25))
```


:::

::: {.column}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-height: 9
# Init the fake data
fake_counts <- tibble(fake_group = c(rep("A", 32), 
                                     rep("B", 16), rep("C", 16)))# Compute the summary with standard errors:
fake_counts |>
  mutate(fake_group = factor(fake_group, levels = c("C", "B", "A"))) |> 
  group_by(fake_group) |>
  summarize(count = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(total = sum(count),
         prop = count / total,
         se = sqrt(prop * (1 - prop) / total),
         lower = prop - qnorm(1 - .05 / 3 / 2) * se,
         upper = prop + qnorm(1 - .05 / 3 / 2) * se) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = fake_group)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "darkblue",
           aes(y = prop),
           stat = "identity") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lower,
                    ymax = upper),
                color = "darkorange",
                width = 0.25, 
                linewidth = 2) +
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = paste0("Bonferroni correction; n = ", nrow(fake_counts))) +
  theme_light() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 32, hjust = 0.5),
        axis.title = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(size = 25))
```

:::
:::
